overview.title: Safeguarding the Breath of Chinatown
overview.description: HEROS — Heat Equity and Resilience in Open Spaces — is a joint research initiative by Tufts University and the Asian Community Development Corporation (ACDC), monitoring heat stress and air quality across 12 open spaces in Boston's Chinatown during Summer 2023. We transform raw environmental sensor data into community-driven insights and policy action.
overview.mapSubtitle: Visualizing thermal stress and air quality across 12 strategic research sites.
overview.policyDesc: Eight research questions organized into four thematic pillars form the analytical backbone of our environmental justice investigation. Each pillar builds on the previous one to construct a rigorous, data-driven narrative for policy action.
overview.missionDesc: Working alongside community members, ACDC, and city partners, Tufts University researchers deployed Kestrel DROP (WBGT, temperature, humidity) and PurpleAir (PM2.5) sensors at 12 open spaces from July–August 2023. The mission: identify which sites face the greatest compound heat and air-quality risk, and equip community leaders and Boston city government with the empirical basis for structural cooling measures and policy action.
overview.advocacy1: Disaggregated WBGT & PM2.5 across 12 open spaces
overview.advocacy2: Identify highest-risk sites for urgent environmental intervention
overview.advocacy3: Share validated findings with community & Boston city government
overview.gateCaption: The Chinatown Paifang — gateway to one of Boston's most environmentally burdened neighborhoods.

q1.title: Calibration Fidelity Ledger
q1.description: This protocol evaluates the operational consistency of PurpleAir PA-II sensors against the MassDEP Federal Equivalent Method (FEM) at the Harrison Ave site. Validation ensures that high-resolution community data maintains scholarly rigor through rigorous regression modeling.
q1.scatterLabel: PA-II vs MassDEP FEM BAM-1022 • 10-min intervals
q1.higherConcentration: Higher concentration trends toward increased sensor divergence.
q1.tempHumidityDesc: Interaction effects become significant above 85°F and moderate humidity.

q2.title: Temperature Sensor Comparison
q2.description: This report evaluates Kestrel ambient temperature measurements across 12 Chinatown open space sites against two reference monitors: the DEP Nubian Square FEM station and the rooftop weather station at 35 Kneeland St. A critical rooftop thermal mass effect is documented.
q2.rooftopLag: The rooftop weather station has a 4-hour thermal lag — an inverted diurnal cycle

q3.title: Distributions & Cumulative Frequency
q3.description: Analysis of PM2.5 and WBGT thresholds across 12 study sites in Boston Chinatown, quantifying public health risk exposure.
q3.dataSource: Data sourced from 12 PurpleAir sensors cross-validated with FEM monitoring stations.

q4.title: AQI & Multi-Pollutant Analysis
q4.description: Daily Air Quality Index and concentrations of CO, SO₂, NO₂, and Ozone in Chinatown based on the MassDEP monitor.
q4.dailyHeatmapDesc: Scholarly review of environmental safety levels (July – August)
q4.corrMatrixDesc: Pearson r between each land-use variable and site-mean PM2.5 / WBGT

q5.title: Hottest Days Analysis
q5.description: Detailed examination of peak thermal stress events across the 12 primary research sites in Boston's Chinatown.
q5.intersiteRangeDesc: Max variance between sites on hot days

q5deep.title: Statistical Deep Dive
q5deep.description: Rigorous statistical validation, ranking consistency, and Heat Index divergence analysis for the 5 hottest days.
q5deep.mediumEffect: Medium effect — hottest vs coolest site
q5deep.siteDistDesc: Distribution of WBGT readings on the 5 hottest days — ordered by mean
q5deep.rankingDesc: How stable is each site's WBGT rank across the 5 hottest days?
q5deep.dayHeatmapDesc: Mean WBGT by site on each of the 5 hottest days

q6.title: Highest AQI Days (PM2.5)
q6.description: Temporal anomaly archive & spatial disparity analysis across Chinatown's 12 research sites.
q6.aqiTimelineDesc: Study period with the 5 highest AQI days highlighted — PM2.5 site spread shown below
q6.deviationDesc: PM2.5 deviation from daily network average across peak AQI episodes
q6.sitePm25Desc: Mean PM2.5 concentration by site on selected day

q7.title: PM2.5 × Heat Stress Correlation
q7.description: Investigating the compound relationship between particulate matter and Wet Bulb Globe Temperature across Chinatown's open spaces.
q7.perDegreeDesc: Each 1°F increase in WBGT associates with a {{slope}} µg/m³ increase in PM2.5
q7.jointDensityDesc: Observation concentration across the WBGT–PM2.5 space
q7.densityDesc: The densest cluster sits at WBGT 64–68°F and PM2.5 4–10 µg/m³
q7.siteHetDesc: Site-level heterogeneity in the PM2.5–WBGT relationship
q7.temporalVariation: Temporal variation in the PM2.5–WBGT relationship

q7het.title: Site-Level Heterogeneity
q7het.description: Why does the PM2.5–WBGT link vary {{fold_diff}}× across Chinatown's monitoring sites? Exploring the spatial inequality in compound environmental risk.
q7het.fourLenses: Four lenses on why the PM2.5–heat link differs across sites
q7het.slopeDesc: How many µg/m³ PM2.5 increase per 1°F WBGT rise at each site

q8.title: Temporal Exposure Patterns
q8.description: Hour-of-day and day-of-week patterns for PM2.5 and WBGT across 12 open-space monitoring sites, revealing compound exposure windows and site-level heterogeneity.
q8.temporalDynamics: The temporal dynamics of urban exposure reveal a critical {{offset_hours}}-hour offset between peak air pollution and thermal stress.
q8.pm25Note: Midday peak observed at solar noon, likely driven by photochemical processing and downward mixing from the residual layer.
q8.wbgtNote: Late afternoon peak reflects cumulative heat storage in urban canyon surfaces and building envelopes.
q8.weekendHigher: Counterintuitively, weekends exhibit a sustained higher PM2.5 baseline.
q8.weekendNote: ~{{delta}} µg/m³ elevation during weekends suggests reduced weekday scavenging by fresh NO emission plumes.

q9.title: Land-Use & Environmental Outcomes
q9.description: Do buffer-level land-cover characteristics — roads, trees, impervious surface — predict PM2.5 and heat stress across Chinatown's monitoring sites?
q9.varianceDesc: PM2.5 variance from road proximity alone
q9.corrRankingDesc: Pearson r with site-mean {{outcome}} — click a bar to explore
q9.regressionDesc: Univariate standardized β for each land-use predictor → {{outcome}}
q9.siteProfilesDesc: Buffer-level land cover (%) and environmental outcome means for each site
q9.synthesisDesc: Road proximity is the single most actionable land-use predictor of PM2.5 — explaining {{pct}}% of inter-site variance.
q9.heatComplexityDesc: WBGT defies simple land-use prediction — 0 significant predictors. Microscale factors (wind corridors, building shade, waterfront proximity) dominate thermal exposure in this dense urban context.
q9.policyActionDesc: Traffic management, buffer vegetation, and pedestrian re-routing at high-road-area sites represent the most evidence-based intervention path for reducing PM2.5 exposure in Chinatown.

q9clust.title: Land-Use Clustering & PCA
q9clust.description: Grouping Chinatown's sites by land-cover profiles — do distinct land-use clusters correspond to different environmental outcomes?
q9clust.corrHeatmapDesc: Pearson r between each land-use variable and site-mean PM2.5 / WBGT. ★ = p < 0.05
q9clust.pcaLoadingsDesc: How each land-use variable contributes to each PC

clustering.title: Site Clustering Analysis
clustering.description: K-means grouping of 12 monitoring sites by environmental profile — PM2.5, temperature, WBGT, and humidity — revealing three distinct site typologies with implications for public health policy.
clustering.radarDesc: Normalized 0–1 across all features. Similar shapes = similar environmental conditions.

community.title: Thermal Justice in Chinatown
community.description: HEROS — Heat Equity and Resilience in Open Spaces — is a Tufts University & ACDC study documenting heat stress and air quality across 12 open spaces in Boston's Chinatown during Summer 2023, with the goal of centering community voices in environmental policy.
community.agingDesc: ~5,500 residents call Chinatown home — approximately 60% Asian and 49% foreign-born, in one of Boston's most densely populated neighborhoods — directly facing compounding heat and air quality hazards. (Source: 2020 U.S. Census / ACS, as reported in HEROS Study 2023)
community.linguisticDesc: Approximately 59% of Chinatown residents speak a language other than English at home, limiting access to emergency heat warnings and public health information. (Source: 2020 ACS, as reported in HEROS Study 2023)
community.uhiTitle: The Cumulative Impact of the Urban Heat Island
community.uhiDesc1: Boston's Chinatown sits at the confluence of I-93 and I-90 — two of the region's busiest highway corridors. Combined with dense impervious surfaces and limited tree canopy, these conditions create a heat island effect that concentrates both thermal stress and particulate pollution across the neighborhood's 12 open spaces.
community.uhiDesc2: HEROS Kestrel DROP sensors recorded a July median WBGT of 67.8°F (max 84.9°F) and an August maximum of 88.0°F across all sites. Castle Square was consistently the hottest; Mary Soo Hoo the coolest. For PM2.5, One Greenway and Lyndboro Park — nearest to highway on-ramps — registered the highest concentrations. Oxford Plaza and Castle Square were the cleanest.
community.residentQuote: "For residents of Chinatown, poor air quality and high heat are really felt as daily challenges."
community.residentQuoteAttrib: — Jenny Huang, Director of Community Design & Programs, ACDC (Tufts Medicine, Feb 2025)
community.herosResearchDesc: Read how Tufts University and ACDC collaborated to quantify heat stress and air pollution across 12 open spaces in Chinatown during Summer 2023.
community.heatResourcesDesc: The City of Boston maintains cooling center maps and heat emergency resources at boston.gov/heat. The EPA's AirNow tracks daily air quality at airnow.gov.
community.acdcDesc: The Asian Community Development Corporation works with Chinatown residents on open space design, urban greening, and long-term climate resilience investment.
